sentences.text: See, I have a lot of testimonies from Auschwitz, from section housing, from Gross-Rosen, because I testified in the trials in Germany.
Three times I was in Germany called as a witness, in the '60s and the '70s, and prosecutors came here.
[INAUDIBLE].
Half rich.
Half rich.
Halbreich.
After being ten days-- oh, not yet.
No, in Auschwitz only.
Mm hm.
Yeah.
As soon as we arrived, they made the selection.
The old people, sick people, they were taking away.
And we remained in the camp.
The first thing on the first day, naturally, taking a shower.
We were tattooed.
And we went in the Block 10-- Block 11, I'm sorry.
Block 11.
And you are familiar with Block 11.
Between Block 11 and 10 was the place where they shot the people.
And we were remaining there doing nothing for 10 days.
They took us down, and they formed a group, a transport.
And we marched about, I would say, three quarters of an hour to an hour, to a new camp.
It was Auschwitz-Buna.
You have to know that Auschwitz consisted of three main camps.
Auschwitz-I, this was administration.
Auschwitz-II was Birkenau, the death camp.
And Auschwitz-III, Buna, was a labor camp.
When we came there we were divided into different blocks-- barracks.
And the next morning at roll call they ask us our profession.
And it turned to me, I'm a pharmacist.
Tomorrow you will go to the hospital to work.
I reported to the hospital.
Naturally, there was no pharmacy where I could actually practice, because there were no medications.
And I got a room in the internal disease department.
I had about in a room half of the size here about thirty-- two to 36 people laying on pneumonia.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What you're doing without medications?
So my thought naturally was, we wetted linens, bed linens, sheets, with hot water and cold water, and we applied it to the bodies.
We changed ten minutes hot, ten minutes cold to reduce the temperature, most of all.
Once in a while-- the administrator of the hospital was naturally a Nazi, a German, a sergeant, a fairly decent man.
He didn't care what we're doing, how we're doing.
So long everything was in order, he didn't worry.
And from time to time he brought us medications.
One day I come in and I see on the table 12 tablets, antibiotics.
So, but what shall I do with 12 tablets?
I have 32 people laying.
To divide them breaks them in sixteenths.
This would mean nothing to it.
So the doctor-- most Jewish doctors, famous professors from all countries, from France, from Belgium, from Czechoslovakia, from Poland, from Germany, from Holland.
They came from all over.
They were prisoners, like me.
And we sat down with the doctor.
Tell me, what shall we do?
So we decided to give it to the youngest ones, who had the biggest chance to survive.
So, again, 12 pills is just enough for one day for one person.
Whom do you give it to?
So we had two or three we wanted to like to save.
So I turned to the SS man again.
I told him, Neubert, you know you gave me 12 pills, 12 tablets.
You know it is for one person, and I have a few young ones.
I would like to save them.
He could take the gun and shoot me just for the question.
But he never said yes, or no, I cannot do it, or I did not bring it, or I will bring it.
Never.
He turned away, saying nothing, and he went out into the SS camp across from our camp.
And after an hour, after two hours, I turned around.
I have a package-- a bag of more medications.
Now there came selections.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A prisoner who got sick, who was admitted to the hospital, could stay no longer than two weeks.
A doctor from the main camp, from Auschwitz, from the main hospital came, Dr.
Mengele once, when he was on duty, or Dr.
Konig, or Doctor-- I forgot the name.
So many coming.
They came and they made selections.

We had to take out all the sick prisoners in front of the barracks, naturally naked, and officer-- beautiful dressed, white gloves-- and he would stood there and only with a finger he pointed out left or right.
The ones who were selected to left, they were separated.
And the right ones, they were able to work it, maybe in a couple days, in three days they were safe.
And afterwards he went out again from the camp.
The SS man, this Neubert, he had the job to register the people who were selected to go to the gas chamber.
And he had the names and the numbers on.
And when we saw between the 120 they selected, we saw some young people, I went again to him.
I said, Neubert, there are two or three I would like to save.
This is my friend's son.
Or, he is from my hometown.
Naturally, he knew I'm lying.
But he crossed out two or three.
He couldn't cross out 120 because he had to deliver the people the doctor selected.
So, again, we saved a couple three.
But now the trucks arrived to take those sick people, the 120 or so, to Auschwitz, to Birkenau, to the gas chamber.
They were so sick already, they couldn't even get up on the truck.
So we were helping them to go on the truck.
Do you know-- it took me years after the liberation, I was fighting with my conscience, I am actually part-- I'm guilty.
I let them-- I helped them going to the gas chamber.
But later on, naturally, I understood.
No, I helped them to make it easier, the few last couple hours or something, you know?
And those people who were already on the truck, they were very sick, even without mind.
Their minds didn't work.
But they still had the strength to take off jacket or the shoes, throw it over the truck, leave it for the other ones who are alive.
See, this is part of the resistance.
Maybe there were exceptions, you know, some little town or something that depicted a Jew or something, but I don't think so.
That's right.
Yeah.
But they had the largest anti-Semitism.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yeah.
And I was in the hospital working about three months, maybe less.
Two months.
And all of a sudden I was assigned to the re-educational camp.
This meant-- re-educational camp consisted of prisoners of non-Jews who worked for the Germans during the war as [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] forced laborers.
And they were brought from all occupied countries to Auschwitz.
And they worked for them in the factory which we had to build-- this Buna Werke.
When they went on [GERMAN], when they extended the [GERMAN]-- they came late, or they run away from work, or they committed sabotage, this was [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] the biggest crime they committed.
They were brought to this camp depending on the crimes they committed.
The least was in months, the worst, for six months.
And [INAUDIBLE], this re-education camp, actually translated is-- re-educational camp is [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] means education.
So everybody thinks it's a school, and you teach them history or languages or something, mathematic.
No.
They were taken to work.
They had to march with all other prisoners, who were there forever, to work at the factory.
But they were separated.
And the separated, they were same thing in the camp.
In this big Buna camp, in front at the gate, there were five barracks separated by an additional fence.
And they put me in charge of.
I don't know why.
From the beginning, my camp oldest in the hospital was a Polish doctor.
And he appointed me to go there to establish an ambulance and take care of the people who come into this camp.
Not in order to bring some of the sick to the main hospital, to us, but I should treat them there.
And it was about a week-- and there was already a camp oldest, a half Jew, a German, a half Jew.
And I started to talk with him-- I had my job.
I established an ambulance, and when people were sick, I treated them.
When they got very sick, you know, seriously sick, I took them to the hospital, doctors and everything.
And a week passed.
And the camp oldest-- I was sleeping with him together in one room-- tells me, Sieg, I'm going to run away.
I'm going to escape.
I said, OK.
He said, would you join me?
I didn't tell him yes or no.
I was thinking about it, if to join him.
And I came to the decision not to, because I told myself, when I escape, and I probably would be able-- I would be lucky enough to escape because of my languages.
I speak Polish.
I speak German.
And he is a German, speaks only German.
And I thought myself, OK, I will be lucky.
I get out, I'm in hiding somewhere, and I can maybe survive the war to the end.
Because we knew already what's going on the front.
The Germans had to retreat since Stalingrad, and the western front wasn't too rosy either.
They were beaten, and the Russians came closer and closer.
Everywhere they came to a camp in Poland, they burnt or destroyed the camp, killed all the people.
But I thought to myself, no, I'm not escaping.
I can myself save myself, but what about all the other ones I could help who suffer-- when they suffer?
I stay in the camp.
And I said to him, no, I'm not going.
I helped him.
I gave him my watch, because I was allowed to carry a watch because I was a doctor, you see.
I gave him my watch.
And one day-- and it's Sunday-- I was in the camp, because every night I was free.
I could go to the main camp, you see.
They allowed me to go because of my arm band.
The Germans see I'm a functionary, so I could go.
And, naturally, where do I go?
To the hospital, and I talk to my friends, to the doctors, to the nurses I know.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on Sunday we had a soccer game.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All camp oldest and all camp barrack oldest, they played against the hospital, soccer.
And I was playing for the hospital because I belonged to the hospital, in spite I was already transferred to the re-educational camp.
And during the day, I saw them every day.
Because when the people went out to work, I sent them to work, I was free.
So I was always in contact with the hospital.
And what we did, one day an SS man, an older man, belonged to the Gestapo.
Not to the SS, because my camp belonged to the Gestapo.
And once in a while they came for inspection.
And he came, and he's sitting down, and I served him lunch.
Because when I started to work in the re-educational camp, I never ate anymore from the kitchen.
I had a doctor with me from Czechoslovakia, a Jewish doctor, who was helping me.
He was my assistant.
And he cooked for us, and we organized food so I never ate anymore from the kitchen.
Pardon?
The food we got from the factory.
They smuggled in food.
They got food from the Poles.
They got food from the English prisoner of wars who worked with us there, but lived in another camp.
They were separated.
And I organized it.
People, my friends, brought me, when they were working there, they brought me the food.
We got once to talk with him.
And I ask him-- Wyczorek was his name.
I said, Wyczorek, from where are you?
He said, I am from Silesia.
I said, you know, Mr.
Wyczorek, you don't have to tell me this because the way you speak, half Polish and half German, a mixture-- this was on the boarder, you see.
So I know.
But from what town, from what city?
Oh, I'm from Tarnowitz.
I spent my youth in Tarnowitz.
I said, what did you do there?
I was a baker.
In which bakery?
He gives me the name.
I said, you know, every morning before we went to school, my older brother and myself, we went to the bakery and we got the fresh-baked goods, bake ware, bring home for breakfast, you know?
But I didn't know him because he worked in the back, in the bakery.
I didn't know him.
And from that time he opened up.
You know, Halbreich, the war is almost over.
We suffer terrible, not only on the front but the population of Germany, nothing to eat.
And the Russians coming closer and closer on the west front.
The Americans enter the war in '41-- no, in '42.
And we are beaten.
And we suffer terrible too.
So every time-- he came every day to me.
And every day I served him food.
We ate together.
He was officially hangman.
You know, Auschwitz, I've mentioned already three main camps, but altogether were 39 branches, all distributed in Silesia.
There were coal mines.
There were steel factories, plants, and they were in wherever they needed people to work, they put in the prisoners of war.
And there were separate camps for girls in ammunition factories.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in coal mines the men were working.
All over.
And he was officially a hangman.
When somebody was sentenced to die, he went from camp to camp, and he pulled the string-- the rope.
He was the hangman.
But I worked with him terrific.
And he tipped me off sometimes.
He said, you know, I heard tomorrow the doctor's coming to make selections.
Fine.
And he walked out.
I went right away to the hospital.
I told my doctors and the nurses, tomorrow the doctor's coming.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Leave all young people, release who are able to work for one day only, see?
So we released them at night, and during the day they went to work.
When the doctor came, made the selections, they were safe, because they didn't take part in the selection, see?
So after night, the evening when they came back from work, we admitted them again to the hospital.
We saved hundreds of people this way.
See, the doctor said after two weeks, regardless, when he didn't recuperate, selection out.
What we did-- some young people, they were already two weeks and they didn't recuperate yet.
So they were due to go out to the gas chamber.
We changed the records.
The wrong name-- another name, another number, or we transferred him in another room, you know, to be a little longer in the hospital.
This was our work.
Our doctors?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] From time to time they got medicine like I got, you see, when I was in the hospital.
In the factory our engineers, they built x-ray machines.
And they built the first electric shock machine.
It was actually discovered in the camps.
And in our hospital the German doctors made experiments.
They brought, from the woman camp, about 12 to 15 young girls, young women, mentally already sick.
They brought them once a week, and they experimented in our hospital.
Pardon?
I once took part in it, yeah.
Yeah.
The shock machine, an instrument, big like an x-ray machine, you know?
And attached, naturally, different wires.
They attached them to the head.
And they starting giving shocks, you know?
And a few shocks.
I have to tell you, some died right away.
And the other ones were still alive, but I don't know if anyone recuperated.
But this was the first actual experiments with electric shocks, which actually is being done now too, till now.
And this was in our hospital.
All other experiments, they were done in the main camp in Auschwitz, not by us.
The experiments Dr.
Mengele did on women, the sexual experiments.
He, for instance, gave shots into eyes.
He tried to change the color of the eyes.
He wanted them all to make blue eyes like the Germans, the Aryan race.
Well, till now, there's an organization of the twins.
He experiment on the twins.
And I'm very active on this organization too, about Mengele.
We had many conferences after the war, in United States and in Israel.
And we still are after Mengele.
We don't think he's dead.
He might have died, maybe yesterday, or two months ago, who knows, maybe last year.
But that time, when it came to the point to say he's dead, he was not dead.
Because there is a dentist, a woman dentist in Brazil, who has his x-rays from his teeth.
And we compared the teeth of the body with these x-rays, and they did not compare.
They didn't identify.
So we think it's not his body.
Now, even less than a month, somebody called me, a woman from Salt Lake City.
It's a habit.
She looks after Mengele-- how he died, where he was, if he's still alive.
And she thinks this is the body of his brother.
So we don't know, but this is separate, yeah.
In Auschwitz this went on-- yes, but I was there in the beginning a week, and I returned from the Sunday, from the football game, from the soccer game.
My block eldest, my camp eldest isn't there.
Well, I laid down.
In the morning he's not there.
I had to give the report to the SS men, the roll call.
And he comes to the camp, and I give him the report, saw so many people-- one is missing.
They knew already that one is missing because [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] the fence was cut, and he escaped.
And he could escape because this spot of the camp, on Sunday when all the people in the camp not going out to work, this was not occupied.
Only the fence was there.
He knew about it.
He did say escape on Sunday.
He went through and disappeared.
They never caught him, and we never knew what happened to him.
But we think he made it to the Poles, in the mountains, but he was killed right away because he didn't speak Polish, only German.
They considered him as a spy and they killed him.
His name?
Rudy Kahn.
He was a German-Jew and half-Jew, and no trace about him.
Yeah.
After a while when the SS men reported that they found the missing one-- I mean, he is from the re-educational camp, the kapo, the block eldest.
I was called to the Gestapo.
And he asked me, said, Halbreich, you did not know that he escaped?
I said, no, you have to realize I'm only a week with him.
And we hardly communicated privately, only officially, what we have to do next day and what's happened and so far.
And then I read a book, and he read a book, and we fall asleep, this was all.
I don't know him even.
I hardly knew him.
This was only one week I'm there.
So he tells me, from now on, you're taking over the camp.
And I became the camp oldest.
And right away I sent-- I asked the hospital to send me a doctor because I cannot take anymore-- help the ambulance.
I have to take care of the camp-- of the whole camp.
And they sent me Dr.
Winter, Alfred Winter from Czechoslovakia.
